ROUND 2 for task **T113** (goals:G6), DOCS-ONLY (docs/install.md — §9 full-tunnel recipe + §5 C6 NAT/forwarding subsection). Your round-1 work is at commit `39d0cb1619ac7917ac96c8e5692afa0ee6ba9a54`. In YOUR isolated worktree: `git reset --hard 39d0cb1619ac7917ac96c8e5692afa0ee6ba9a54` (sanctioned), then `git switch -c t113-fix`. Report `branch: t113-fix` + resultCommit.

The review panel UNANIMOUSLY DISAPPROVED. Fix all of these (docs/install.md only). The SNAT-branch primary recipe was verified sound — do NOT rewrite it; only fix the specific inconsistencies:

**Criticism 1 (both reviewers): §9 intro bold warning self-contradicts.** Your §9 opening says in bold "**Never write a literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]`** (D35, open: it wedges the WG handshake permanently)". But §9.1's own TOML block twelve lines later writes exactly `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]`, and §3 (verified in code: splitDefaultRoute in internal/device/device.go uapiConfig runs UNCONDITIONALLY) states a config-literal /0 is ALWAYS split to /1+/1 at UAPI render, so it is SAFE at the config level. The real invariant is the ENGINE-BOUNDARY one: the daemon guarantees the engine never receives a literal /0 (D35 tracks the underlying engine defect that would wedge IF a raw /0 reached it). Reword the warning to state the actual invariant — config-level 0.0.0.0/0 is safe because the daemon splits it; do NOT forbid in bold the exact config §9.1 then prescribes. (Alternatively switch §9.1's example to the explicit /1+/1 pair — but the reword is cleaner and matches §3.)

**Criticism 2 (both reviewers): §9.2 widen-allowed_ips alternative breaks the C6 MASQUERADE.** You present "widen the concentrator's allowed_ips instead of SNAT" as an equivalent substitute, but §5 C6's `MASQUERADE -s <tunnel-net>` (10.77.0.0/24) will NOT match traffic that arrives un-SNAT'd sourced from the client subnet (192.168.223.0/24 ∉ 10.77.0.0/24) — so full-tunnel egress leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source and the return path dies. Yet §9.3 says C6's items "are all required for this recipe" with no branch adjustment. Fix: add a sentence to the widen-allowed_ips alternative stating that this branch ADDITIONALLY requires widening the concentrator's `MASQUERADE -s` to also cover the client subnet (or use a supernet covering both), and note that (unlike the SNAT branch) this branch was NOT validated on the Pi/o3 deploy.

**Criticism 3: §9.2 SNAT-address pointer misdirects.** Your §9.2 says to substitute "the edge's `allowed_ips` address from its own peer config, §3". But in the edge's OWN config, the peer `allowed_ips` holds the CONCENTRATOR's tunnel address (10.77.0.1/32, §3 examples ~install.md:96/584). The SNAT target is the edge's OWN tunnel IP (10.77.0.2), which comes from the edge's §4 interface `Address=` (~install.md:824) — NOT the peer allowed_ips. As written, an operator would SNAT to 10.77.0.1, which the concentrator's cryptokey routing (allowed_ips 10.77.0.2/32 for this peer) silently drops. Point at §4's `Address=` instead.

READ the relevant install.md sections (§3, §4, §5 C6, §9) + internal/device/device.go splitDefaultRoute/uapiConfig + wanbond-fixes.md to ground the corrections. Change ONLY docs/install.md.

Gate before commit: `nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...'`. Commit on `t113-fix`. Return the standard structured result JSON {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary}. Do NOT mutate the ledger, do NOT merge. End with a `### Session summary` block.

TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.

TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

content.content:
1110	## 9. Full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (C3)
1111	
1112	wanbond's primary use case: route an entire client LAN's traffic through the
1113	bonded tunnel and out the concentrator's public IP. This is end-to-end
1114	validated on the production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md`): edge = a
1115	Raspberry Pi with two WAN uplinks arriving as VLAN sub-interfaces (one per
1116	WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
1117	concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
1118	addresses/interfaces throughout.
1119	
1120	**Never write a literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` (D35, open: it wedges
1121	the WG handshake permanently — see §3's cross-field constraints).** Both
1122	routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the whole
1123	internet through the tunnel — without ever handing the engine a literal
1124	`/0`:
1125	
1126	### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)
1127	
1128	Set `mode = "default-route"` on the concentrator peer alongside a
1129	`0.0.0.0/0` (and, for IPv6, `::/0`) `allowed_ips` entry:
1130	
1131	```toml
1132	[[wireguard.peers]]
1133	public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
1134	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"
1135	allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
1136	mode = "default-route"             # see §3: edge-only, full-tunnel opt-in
1137	```
1138	
1139	The daemon splits `0.0.0.0/0` into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair
1140	(`0.0.0.0/1` + `128.0.0.0/1`) at UAPI render regardless of `mode` — so the
1141	engine itself never sees the literal `/0` that wedges D35 — and, because
1142	`mode = "default-route"` is set, ALSO installs that same split as scope-link
1143	routes via `wanbond0` once the interface comes up, withdrawing them on stop.
1144	This is the daemon's **only** route programming anywhere in this recipe;
1145	everything below (9.2–9.3) is entirely operator-owned, same boundary as §3/§4.
1146	
1147	If you would rather not opt into `mode` at all, install the equivalent
1148	routes yourself instead (e.g. from the addressing oneshot, §4):
1149	
1150	```sh
1151	sudo ip route add 0.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
1152	sudo ip route add 128.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
1153	```
1154	
1155	### 9.2 Edge: route the client LAN onto wanbond0 (operator-owned)
1156	
1157	Policy-route the client subnet to `wanbond0` and **SNAT to the edge's own
1158	tunnel address**, so the concentrator's per-edge `allowed_ips =
1159	<edge-tunnel-ip>/32` (the ordinary point-to-point form, §3) still matches
1160	the source address of forwarded traffic — no widening needed on the
1161	concentrator side:
1162	
1163	```sh
1164	sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
1165	sudo ip rule add from 192.168.223.0/24 lookup 223
1166	sudo ip route add default dev wanbond0 table 223
1167	sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to-source 10.77.0.2
1168	```
1169	
1170	(`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
1171	client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
1172	§C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the

## 9. Full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (C3)

wanbond's primary use case: route an entire client LAN's traffic through the
bonded tunnel and out the concentrator's public IP. This is end-to-end
validated on the production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md`): edge = a
Raspberry Pi with two WAN uplinks arriving as VLAN sub-interfaces (one per
WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
addresses/interfaces throughout.

**Never write a literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` (D35, open: it wedges
the WG handshake permanently — see §3's cross-field constraints).** Both
routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the whole
internet through the tunnel — without ever handing the engine a literal
`/0`:

### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)

Set `mode = "default-route"` on the concentrator peer alongside a
`0.0.0.0/0` (and, for IPv6, `::/0`) `allowed_ips` entry:

```toml
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"
allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
mode = "default-route"             # see §3: edge-only, full-tunnel opt-in
```

The daemon splits `0.0.0.0/0` into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair
(`0.0.0.0/1` + `128.0.0.0/1`) at UAPI render regardless of `mode` — so the
engine itself never sees the literal `/0` that wedges D35 — and, because
`mode = "default-route"` is set, ALSO installs that same split as scope-link
routes via `wanbond0` once the interface comes up, withdrawing them on stop.
This is the daemon's **only** route programming anywhere in this recipe;
everything below (9.2–9.3) is entirely operator-owned, same boundary as §3/§4.

If you would rather not opt into `mode` at all, install the equivalent
routes yourself instead (e.g. from the addressing oneshot, §4):

```sh
sudo ip route add 0.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
sudo ip route add 128.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
```

### 9.2 Edge: route the client LAN onto wanbond0 (operator-owned)

Policy-route the client subnet to `wanbond0` and **SNAT to the edge's own
tunnel address**, so the concentrator's per-edge `allowed_ips =
<edge-tunnel-ip>/32` (the ordinary point-to-point form, §3) still matches
the source address of forwarded traffic — no widening needed on the
concentrator side:

```sh
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
sudo ip rule add from 192.168.223.0/24 lookup 223
sudo ip route add default dev wanbond0 table 223
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to-source 10.77.0.2
```

(`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
§C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the
edge's `allowed_ips` address from its own peer config, §3.)

**Alternative — widen the concentrator's `allowed_ips` instead of SNAT-ing on
the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
`allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32", "192.168.223.0/24"]` and skip the edge-side
`iptables -t nat` step above. Trades a wider `allowed_ips` (the concentrator
now cryptokey-routes the client subnet directly to this peer) for one fewer
operator-owned step; pick one form, not both.

Put the `ip_forward` toggle, the `ip rule`/`ip route`, and (if used) the
`iptables -t nat` SNAT rule into the edge's addressing oneshot
(`wanbond-addressing@edge.service`, §4) — `wanbond0` and everything routed
through it are torn down and recreated on every daemon restart unless
`tun_persist = true` (§4, I7), so these must be re-applied the same way the
existing persistence recipe re-applies addressing.

### 9.3 Concentrator: NAT and forward the tunnel traffic out the WAN (operator-owned)

See §5 "Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)"
— `ip_forward`, `MASQUERADE`, and the `FORWARD` established/related accept
are all required for this recipe and, like 9.2, are entirely
operator-owned: the daemon programs none of them (§3's `mode` boundary
covers routes only, never NAT/forwarding/policy-routing).

### 9.4 Persistence

Both the edge's policy-route/SNAT rules (9.2) and the concentrator's
NAT/forward rules (§5 C6) reference `wanbond0` or its addresses and so must
survive both reboots and daemon restarts. Use the existing §4
`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` oneshot for anything keyed to the
interface (9.2's `ip rule`/`ip route`, and the `iptables -t nat` SNAT rule
if you took the SNAT branch above), and `netfilter-persistent save` (§5) for
the concentrator's `iptables`/`ip6tables` rules, which persist independently
of the interface. `net.ipv4.ip_forward` persists on its own once written to
`/etc/sysctl.d/` (§5 C6).

41	## 3. Write the config file — 0600 REQUIRED
42	
43	The daemon takes exactly one flag: `--config <path>`. The systemd units use
44	`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml` and `/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`.
45	
46	The file holds the WireGuard private key and the outer-control PSK, so
47	`config.Load` **refuses any file whose permission bits are not exactly `0600`**
48	(`insecure permissions` error at startup). Create it as:
49	
50	```sh
51	mkdir -p /etc/wanbond
52	touch /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
53	chown root:root /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
54	chmod 0600 /etc/wanbond/edge.toml
55	```
56	
57	### Edge config (`/etc/wanbond/edge.toml`)
58	
59	```toml
60	role = "edge"
61	psk = "<base64 32-byte outer-control PSK, same on both ends>"
62	
63	# One [[paths]] block per WAN uplink. source_addr pins the path's UDP socket
64	# to the local source IP the upstream router routes out the intended WAN.
65	[[paths]]
66	name = "starlink"
67	source_addr = "192.168.1.10"
68	
69	[[paths]]
70	name = "5g"
71	source_addr = "192.168.2.10"
72	# dest_addr = "203.0.113.7:51820"  # optional per-path concentrator endpoint;
73	                                   # omit when one public IP fronts all uplinks
74	
75	[wireguard]
76	private_key = "<base64 edge private key>"
77	
78	[[wireguard.peers]]
79	public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
80	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"     # required on the edge; an ip:port literal
81	                                   # (default) OR a hostname:port behind the
82	                                   # opt-in `dns = true` below — see §Optional
83	                                   # [dns] resolver block.
84	# dns = true                        # OPTIONAL, default false. Enables hostname
85	# resolution for the endpoint above (e.g. endpoint = "hub.example.com:51820").
86	# The [dns] block is itself OPTIONAL: absent, hostnames resolve through the
87	# OS system resolver; see §Optional [dns] resolver block below.
88	# endpoints = ["203.0.113.7:51820", "198.51.100.7:51820"]  # ordered hub-failover
89	# form (Q18): first entry is the active/primary concentrator, the rest are
90	# ordered standbys tried in order when the active one is lost. Mutually
91	# exclusive with `endpoint` above — `endpoint` is just its one-element form,
92	# so a single-concentrator deployment keeps using it unchanged. Edge-only.
93	# Each entry may likewise be an ip:port literal or, with `dns = true`, a
94	# hostname:port. Hub failover (all-paths-down detection + switch/re-handshake
95	# to the next endpoint) is implemented: T54 config surface + T57 switch.
96	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.1/32"]     # concentrator's inner tunnel address
97	
98	[metrics]
99	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"          # loopback only; the daemon rejects anything else
100	
101	[log]
102	level = "info"
103	```
104	
105	### Concentrator config (`/etc/wanbond/concentrator.toml`)
106	
107	```toml
108	role = "concentrator"
109	psk = "<same PSK as the edge>"
110	
111	# The concentrator learns the edge's real per-path endpoints from
112	# authenticated traffic; it only needs its own bind address.
113	[[paths]]
114	name = "wan0"
115	source_addr = "10.0.0.5"           # the VPS's primary (private) interface IP
116	
117	[wireguard]
118	private_key = "<base64 concentrator private key>"
119	listen_port = 51820                # required on the concentrator
120	
121	[[wireguard.peers]]
122	public_key = "<base64 edge public key>"
123	allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32"]     # edge's inner tunnel address; no endpoint —
124	                                   # the concentrator roams the edge dynamically
125	
126	[metrics]
127	listen = "127.0.0.1:9090"
128	
129	[log]
130	level = "info"
131	```
132	
133	Optional on both ends: an `[amnezia]` obfuscation block (all-or-nothing — when
134	used, `jc`, `jmin`, `jmax`, `s1`, `s2` must all be set, identically on both
135	ends). Omit it entirely for plain WireGuard framing.
136	
137	Generate keys with standard WireGuard tooling (`wg genkey | tee k | wg pubkey`)
138	and the PSK with `head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64`.
139

141	
142	A concentrator can bond traffic from **multiple edges** (e.g., a branch-office
143	edge, a mobile edge, a cloud gateway). Plural `[[wireguard.peers]]` blocks are
144	supported; with more than one peer, each edge authenticates with its own psk
145	for authenticated source-based demux:
146	
147	```toml
148	role = "concentrator"
149	psk = "<base64 32-byte PSK>"       # REQUIRED by validation; authenticates no
150	                                   # peer once a second peer is configured

795	### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
796	
797	The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it
798	administratively **UP** itself (`SIOCSIFFLAGS`/`IFF_UP`) right after creation —
799	so a write to it never silently fails with `EIO` for want of a link-up step —
800	but otherwise owns ONLY the tunnel engine: **it never assigns addresses**, and
801	**installs no routes** — with ONE narrow exception: a peer marked
802	`mode = "default-route"` (see §3) makes the daemon install that peer's
803	`allowed_ips` split (the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair for `0.0.0.0/0`) as scope-link
804	routes via `wanbond0`, and remove them on stop. That exception is routes ONLY —
805	no policy routing, SNAT, or concentrator forwarding, which remain operator-owned
806	(put them in the addressing script below). Addressing is always operator-owned (wg-quick
807	style, no privileged shell-outs); assign it with a **systemd-networkd `.network`
808	file**, using the inner addresses from `allowed_ips`:
809	
810	If something ELSE takes the link back down after boot (NetworkManager without
811	the unmanaged-devices drop-in below is the common case, D39), a `TUN write`
812	`EIO` no longer surfaces only as the engine's raw `input/output error`: the
813	daemon inspects the interface's link state and MTU and logs an actionable
814	ERROR naming the cause (e.g. `wanbond0 is DOWN — address & bring it up
815	(install.md §4)`) alongside the raw errno, rate-limited to one diagnostic per
816	30s so a write storm does not flood the log.
817	
818	```ini
819	# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
820	[Match]
821	Name=wanbond0
822	
823	[Network]
824	Address=10.77.0.2/24
825	```
826	
827	```sh
828	systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
829	```
830	
831	Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
832	drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
833	the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
834	and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
835	with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
836	unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
837	watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
838	appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
839	there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)
840	
841	#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)
842	
843	By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
844	the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
845	it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
846	dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
847	any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.
848	
849	Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
850	issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
851	stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
852	device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
853	referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
854	`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
855	being torn down on stop.
856	
857	> **NetworkManager (D39):** a *persistent* `wanbond0` still needs the
858	> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
859	> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
860	> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
861	> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
862	> `tun_persist`.
863	
864	#### Persistence recipe for non-networkd hosts (`wanbond-addressing@.service`)
865	
866	The `systemd-networkd` `.network` recipe above needs `systemd-networkd`
867	itself watching `wanbond0`. On a host that addresses the interface another
868	way — NetworkManager with the unmanaged-devices drop-in above but no
869	networkd `.network` file, or neither daemon running — use the shipped
870	templated oneshot instead, `packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service`
871	(instance = role: `edge` or `concentrator`):
872	
873	```sh
874	cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service /etc/systemd/system/
875	# Write your own re-apply script (address + link-up + policy rules +
876	# per-table routes + nft SNAT — whatever the role needs) and, optionally, an
877	# environment file it can read:
878	$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh    # or addressing-concentrator.sh
879	chmod +x /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh
880	$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.env   # optional EnvironmentFile
881	systemctl daemon-reload
882	systemctl enable --now wanbond-addressing@edge.service   # instance = role
883	```
884	
885	`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` is `PartOf=`/`After=wanbond-<role>.service`
886	(stops with the daemon, only ever starts after it) and has
887	`ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh` — leave that script
888	unwritten and the unit is skipped, not failed. It is a `Type=oneshot` with
889	`RemainAfterExit=yes` that runs `ExecStart=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh`,
890	optionally fed by `EnvironmentFile=-/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.env`.
891	
892	**Do not replace its `ExecStartPre` wait loop with a plain `ExecStartPost=` on
893	`wanbond-<role>.service` itself.** That is the exact race R27 found and fixed:
894	`wanbond-edge.service`/`wanbond-concentrator.service` are `Type=exec`, so
895	systemd considers them started the instant `execve()` returns — **before**
896	the daemon has created `wanbond0` — so an `ExecStartPost` there runs against a
897	not-yet-existent interface, fails with "Cannot find device", and (being
898	un-prefixed) fails the unit and trips the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop
899	(the same failure mode as the raw `ip address add` `ExecStartPost` warned
900	against above). `wanbond-addressing@.service` avoids this by ordering itself
901	`After=` the daemon unit **and** actively polling `/sys/class/net/wanbond0` in
902	its own `ExecStartPre` until the interface exists, so it never races tun
903	creation regardless of how long the daemon takes to create it.
904	
905	Once `tun_persist = true` (above) is enabled, `wanbond0` and the
906	addresses/routes/rules attached to it survive a daemon restart on their own,
907	so re-running this oneshot on every restart becomes **optional but
908	harmless** — keep it enabled anyway as a safety net for what `tun_persist`
909	does not cover (first boot, a manual `ip link delete wanbond0`, a host that
910	cannot enable `tun_persist`).
911	
912	## 5. Firewall
913	
914	### Concentrator: UDP listen port

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
```

Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it
watches for the interface and applies the address the moment `wanbond0`
appears, whenever that is. (The daemon exposes no `sd_notify` readiness, so
there is no ordering guarantee to hang an `ExecStartPost` on.)

#### Persisting `wanbond0` across daemon restarts (`tun_persist`, I7)

By default `wanbond0` is a **non-persistent** TUN: the kernel destroys it when
the daemon's last file descriptor closes on stop, so every restart re-creates
it from scratch and the operator-owned addresses/routes/rules attached to it are
dropped. networkd re-applies its `.network` address on the fresh interface, but
any manual `ip` state and the interface's `ifindex` do not survive.

Set the top-level `tun_persist = true` to opt into persistence: the daemon
issues `TUNSETPERSIST` on start, so the kernel keeps `wanbond0` across a daemon
stop/start with the **same `ifindex`**, and the next start re-adopts the same
device by name rather than recreating it. Addresses, routes, and rules
referencing `wanbond0` then survive a restart untouched. Reverting to
`tun_persist = false` and restarting clears the flag, so the device returns to
being torn down on stop.

> **NetworkManager (D39):** a *persistent* `wanbond0` still needs the
> NetworkManager `unmanaged-devices` drop-in on NM-managed hosts. Persistence
> keeps the link alive across restarts; it does **not** exempt the interface
> from NetworkManager, which would otherwise flush the addresses/routes the
> operator assigned. Keep the unmanaged-devices drop-in in place regardless of
> `tun_persist`.

#### Persistence recipe for non-networkd hosts (`wanbond-addressing@.service`)

The `systemd-networkd` `.network` recipe above needs `systemd-networkd`
itself watching `wanbond0`. On a host that addresses the interface another
way — NetworkManager with the unmanaged-devices drop-in above but no
networkd `.network` file, or neither daemon running — use the shipped
templated oneshot instead, `packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service`
(instance = role: `edge` or `concentrator`):

```sh
cp packaging/systemd/wanbond-addressing@.service /etc/systemd/system/
# Write your own re-apply script (address + link-up + policy rules +
# per-table routes + nft SNAT — whatever the role needs) and, optionally, an
# environment file it can read:
$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh    # or addressing-concentrator.sh
chmod +x /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.sh
$EDITOR /etc/wanbond/addressing-edge.env   # optional EnvironmentFile
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable --now wanbond-addressing@edge.service   # instance = role
```

`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` is `PartOf=`/`After=wanbond-<role>.service`
(stops with the daemon, only ever starts after it) and has
`ConditionPathExists=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh` — leave that script
unwritten and the unit is skipped, not failed. It is a `Type=oneshot` with
`RemainAfterExit=yes` that runs `ExecStart=/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.sh`,
optionally fed by `EnvironmentFile=-/etc/wanbond/addressing-<role>.env`.

**Do not replace its `ExecStartPre` wait loop with a plain `ExecStartPost=` on
`wanbond-<role>.service` itself.** That is the exact race R27 found and fixed:
`wanbond-edge.service`/`wanbond-concentrator.service` are `Type=exec`, so
systemd considers them started the instant `execve()` returns — **before**
the daemon has created `wanbond0` — so an `ExecStartPost` there runs against a
not-yet-existent interface, fails with "Cannot find device", and (being
un-prefixed) fails the unit and trips the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop
(the same failure mode as the raw `ip address add` `ExecStartPost` warned
against above). `wanbond-addressing@.service` avoids this by ordering itself
`After=` the daemon unit **and** actively polling `/sys/class/net/wanbond0` in
its own `ExecStartPre` until the interface exists, so it never races tun
creation regardless of how long the daemon takes to create it.

Once `tun_persist = true` (above) is enabled, `wanbond0` and the
addresses/routes/rules attached to it survive a daemon restart on their own,
so re-running this oneshot on every restart becomes **optional but
harmless** — keep it enabled anyway as a safety net for what `tun_persist`
does not cover (first boot, a manual `ip link delete wanbond0`, a host that
cannot enable `tun_persist`).

### Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)

Required **only** when the concentrator routes traffic on beyond itself — any
full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (§9). Skip this subsection for a plain
point-to-point tunnel that never carries traffic addressed past the tunnel
endpoints themselves. All three items are **operator-owned** — the daemon
programs none of `ip_forward`, NAT, or firewall rules — and match the
production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md` §C3/C6).

1. **`ip_forward` — REQUIRED.** Off by default on most distros; without it the
   kernel never routes tunnel packets on to the WAN, full stop:

   ```sh
   sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
   echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-wanbond-forward.conf
   ```

2. **`MASQUERADE` the tunnel network out the WAN interface — REQUIRED.**
   `<tunnel-net>` is the inner WireGuard subnet (e.g. `10.77.0.0/24`);
   `<wan>` is the concentrator's actual internet-facing NIC — **not**
   `wanbond0` (on o3 this is the private `enp0s6` that OCI itself NATs to the
   public IP; substitute your own WAN-facing interface):

   ```sh
   sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s <tunnel-net> -o <wan> -j MASQUERADE
   ```

3. **`FORWARD`: accept both directions — REQUIRED, easy to miss.** The
   "Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT" rule above (`-i wanbond0 ACCEPT`)
   is an **`INPUT`** rule; it only covers traffic *terminating on* the
   concentrator and says nothing about `FORWARD`, which is what packets pass
   through when the concentrator merely routes them on to the WAN and back.
   The default `FORWARD` policy is `REJECT`/`DROP` on the same distros/clouds
   called out above (OCI included), so it silently drops one direction unless
   both of these are present:

   ```sh
   # outbound leg: client-subnet traffic entering FORWARD via wanbond0
   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
   # return leg: the outbound-leg ACCEPT above covers only that ONE
   # direction — without this conntrack-ESTABLISHED accept too, the default
   # FORWARD REJECT/DROP silently drops all RETURN traffic and the tunnel
   # looks one-way-dead from the client's perspective
   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -o wanbond0 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
   ```

**Persistence.** All of the above (the `sysctl.d` drop-in aside) are
runtime-only mutations, same as the tunnel-interface ACCEPT above — persist
them with the identical `netfilter-persistent` flow already covered in
"Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots" (this section):

```sh
sudo netfilter-persistent save
```

`net.ipv4.ip_forward` written to `/etc/sysctl.d/` (step 1) is already
persistent across reboots without further action (`sysctl --system` re-reads
`/etc/sysctl.d/` on boot).

## 9. Full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (C3)

wanbond's primary use case: route an entire client LAN's traffic through the
bonded tunnel and out the concentrator's public IP. This is end-to-end
validated on the production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md`): edge = a
Raspberry Pi with two WAN uplinks arriving as VLAN sub-interfaces (one per
WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
addresses/interfaces throughout.

**A config-level literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` is safe to write** —
the daemon unconditionally splits it into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair at UAPI
render (§3's `splitDefaultRoute`), so the *engine* itself never receives the
literal `/0` that would otherwise wedge the WG handshake permanently (D35,
open: the underlying engine defect this daemon-side split works around).
Both routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the
whole internet through the tunnel — by relying on that daemon-guaranteed
split, never a raw `/0` at the engine boundary:

### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)

Set `mode = "default-route"` on the concentrator peer alongside a
`0.0.0.0/0` (and, for IPv6, `::/0`) `allowed_ips` entry:

```toml
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"
allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
mode = "default-route"             # see §3: edge-only, full-tunnel opt-in
```

The daemon splits `0.0.0.0/0` into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair
(`0.0.0.0/1` + `128.0.0.0/1`) at UAPI render regardless of `mode` — so the
engine itself never sees the literal `/0` that wedges D35 — and, because
`mode = "default-route"` is set, ALSO installs that same split as scope-link
routes via `wanbond0` once the interface comes up, withdrawing them on stop.
This is the daemon's **only** route programming anywhere in this recipe;
everything below (9.2–9.3) is entirely operator-owned, same boundary as §3/§4.

If you would rather not opt into `mode` at all, install the equivalent
routes yourself instead (e.g. from the addressing oneshot, §4):

```sh
sudo ip route add 0.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
sudo ip route add 128.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
```

### 9.2 Edge: route the client LAN onto wanbond0 (operator-owned)

Policy-route the client subnet to `wanbond0` and **SNAT to the edge's own
tunnel address**, so the concentrator's per-edge `allowed_ips =
<edge-tunnel-ip>/32` (the ordinary point-to-point form, §3) still matches
the source address of forwarded traffic — no widening needed on the
concentrator side:

```sh
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
sudo ip rule add from 192.168.223.0/24 lookup 223
sudo ip route add default dev wanbond0 table 223
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to-source 10.77.0.2
```

(`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
§C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the
edge's own tunnel address from its interface `Address=` in §4. **Not** the
`allowed_ips` in the edge's own peer config, §3 — that holds the
*concentrator's* tunnel address as seen from the edge (e.g. `10.77.0.1/32`),
not the edge's own.)

**Alternative — widen the concentrator's `allowed_ips` instead of SNAT-ing on
the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
`allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32", "192.168.223.0/24"]` and skip the edge-side
`iptables -t nat` step above. Trades a wider `allowed_ips` (the concentrator
now cryptokey-routes the client subnet directly to this peer) for one fewer
operator-owned step; pick one form, not both.

This branch changes what source address reaches the concentrator's WAN NIC:
without the edge-side SNAT, forwarded packets keep their original
client-subnet source (`192.168.223.0/24`), which the §9.3 / §5 C6
`MASQUERADE -s <tunnel-net>` rule (scoped to the tunnel subnet, e.g.
`10.77.0.0/24`) does **not** match — so this branch additionally requires
widening that concentrator `MASQUERADE -s` to also cover the client subnet
(or replacing it with a supernet covering both), otherwise the reply traffic
leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source and the return path dies. Unlike the
SNAT branch above, this alternative has **not** been validated on the
production Pi/o3 deploy.

Put the `ip_forward` toggle, the `ip rule`/`ip route`, and (if used) the
`iptables -t nat` SNAT rule into the edge's addressing oneshot
(`wanbond-addressing@edge.service`, §4) — `wanbond0` and everything routed
through it are torn down and recreated on every daemon restart unless
`tun_persist = true` (§4, I7), so these must be re-applied the same way the
existing persistence recipe re-applies addressing.

### 9.3 Concentrator: NAT and forward the tunnel traffic out the WAN (operator-owned)

See §5 "Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)"
— `ip_forward`, `MASQUERADE`, and the `FORWARD` established/related accept
